knowledgeContent: Understand the purpose and structure of the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO). Know its role in standardizing international aviation regulations.
Describe the role of the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) in regulating aviation safety. Know its relationship with national aviation authorities.
Identify the functions of national aviation authorities (e.g., CAA, DGAC). Understand their role in enforcing aviation regulations.
Explain the significance of the Chicago Convention in international aviation law. Know the key principles established by the Convention.
List the key ICAO Annexes relevant to general aviation. Understand their applicability to PPL/SPL operations.
Identify the main EU regulations governing general aviation (e.g., Regulation (EU) No 965/2012). Understand their scope and applicability.
Describe how national aviation laws complement EU regulations. Know where to find relevant national aviation legislation.
Explain the requirements for aircraft registration. Know the format and significance of registration markings.
Describe the purpose and validity of a Certificate of Airworthiness. Know the conditions under which it remains valid.
Understand the basic requirements for aircraft noise certification. Know its relevance to general aviation operations.
List the types of pilot licenses and ratings recognized by EASA. Understand the privileges and limitations of a PPL/SPL.
Describe the medical requirements for PPL/SPL holders. Know the validity periods of different medical certificates.
Explain the conditions for maintaining license validity. Understand the process for license renewal.
List the required documents to be carried on board an aircraft. Understand their purpose and validity requirements.
Describe the types of logbooks required for aircraft maintenance. Know the entries that must be recorded.
Explain the purpose and applicability of Airworthiness Directives (ADs). Know how to check for applicable ADs.
Describe the basic maintenance requirements for general aviation aircraft. Understand the role of approved maintenance organizations.
Explain the general rules of the air as defined by ICAO. Understand their application in different flight conditions.
Describe the right-of-way rules for different aircraft categories. Know the exceptions and special cases.
Identify the light signals used by ATC and other aircraft. Understand their meaning and required pilot responses.
Explain the basic requirements for VFR flight. Know the visibility and cloud clearance minima for different airspace classes.
Describe the basic requirements for IFR flight. Understand the differences between VFR and IFR operations.
List the ICAO airspace classes and their characteristics. Understand the VFR requirements for each class.
Describe the requirements for operating in controlled airspace. Know the communication and clearance procedures.
Identify different types of special use airspace (e.g., restricted, danger areas). Understand the restrictions and procedures for flying near them.
Explain the purpose of Aerodrome Traffic Zones (ATZ) and Control Zones (CTR). Know the procedures for entering and operating within them.
Describe the structure of Terminal Control Areas (TMA) and Flight Information Regions (FIR). Understand their significance for flight planning.
Explain when a flight plan is required for VFR flights. Know the information included in a standard flight plan.
Describe the types of ATC clearances and their importance. Understand how to read back and comply with clearances.
List the standard radio communication procedures for VFR flights. Know the phraseology for common ATC communications.
Describe the radio procedures for emergency situations. Know the meaning of MAYDAY and PAN-PAN calls.
Identify the standard markings used on aerodromes. Understand their significance for taxiing and runway operations.
Describe the different types of aerodrome lighting systems. Know their colors and meanings.
Explain the standard procedures for visual approaches. Understand the importance of traffic pattern discipline.
Describe common noise abatement procedures at aerodromes. Know how to apply them during takeoff and landing.
List the different types of air traffic services (ATS). Understand their roles and responsibilities.
Describe the purpose of Flight Information Services (FIS). Know how to obtain and use FIS during flight.
Explain the sources of meteorological information for flight planning. Understand the importance of obtaining updated weather data.
Describe the purpose and content of NOTAMs. Know how to access and interpret them.
Identify common flight restrictions (e.g., temporary flight restrictions). Understand the reasons for their implementation.
Explain the concept of prohibited areas and their significance. Know the penalties for violating airspace restrictions.
Describe the procedures for reporting aviation accidents and incidents. Know the legal obligations of pilots.
Explain the purpose of mandatory occurrence reporting systems. Understand what types of events must be reported.
Describe basic aviation security measures for general aviation. Know the procedures for handling security threats.
List items prohibited on board aircraft. Understand the reasons for these restrictions.
Explain the environmental regulations affecting aviation. Know the restrictions on fuel venting and noise emissions.
Describe the procedures for wildlife hazard management at aerodromes. Understand the risks posed by bird strikes.
Explain the basic principles of aviation liability and insurance. Know the minimum insurance requirements for PPL/SPL operations.
Describe the basic rights of passengers in general aviation. Understand the obligations of pilots towards passengers.
Explain the requirements for conducting international flights under VFR. Know the documentation needed for cross-border operations.
Describe the customs and immigration procedures for international flights. Know the designated airports of entry.
Explain how human factors influence compliance with air law. Understand the consequences of non-compliance.
Describe the legal responsibilities of a PPL/SPL holder. Understand the importance of adhering to regulations.

text: Identify the main components of an aircraft fuselage.
knowledgeContent: The fuselage is the main body of the aircraft, housing the cockpit, passengers, and cargo. It is designed to withstand aerodynamic forces and provide structural integrity.

text: Describe the function of aircraft wings.
knowledgeContent: Wings generate lift to support the aircraft in flight. They also house fuel tanks and provide mounting points for control surfaces.

text: Explain the role of the empennage in aircraft stability.
knowledgeContent: The empennage includes the tail section, which provides stability and control through the horizontal and vertical stabilizers.

text: Identify the types of landing gear configurations.
knowledgeContent: Landing gear can be tricycle, tailwheel, or other configurations, designed to support the aircraft during takeoff, landing, and ground operations.

text: Explain the materials commonly used in airframe construction.
knowledgeContent: Airframes are typically constructed from aluminum alloys, composites, or steel, chosen for their strength, weight, and corrosion resistance.

text: Describe the purpose of wing spars and ribs.
knowledgeContent: Wing spars provide structural strength, while ribs maintain the wing’s aerodynamic shape and support the skin.

text: Describe the primary flight controls and their functions.
knowledgeContent: Primary flight controls include the ailerons, elevator, and rudder, which manage roll, pitch, and yaw respectively.

text: Explain the purpose of secondary flight controls.
knowledgeContent: Secondary flight controls, such as flaps and slats, enhance lift and drag characteristics during takeoff and landing.

text: Describe the function of trim systems.
knowledgeContent: Trim systems reduce pilot workload by maintaining desired aircraft attitude without continuous control input.

text: Explain the operation of ailerons in roll control.
knowledgeContent: Ailerons move differentially to create lift asymmetry, causing the aircraft to roll around its longitudinal axis.

text: Describe the function of the elevator in pitch control.
knowledgeContent: The elevator controls pitch by changing the angle of the horizontal stabilizer, altering the aircraft’s nose-up or nose-down attitude.

text: Explain the role of the rudder in yaw control.
knowledgeContent: The rudder controls yaw by deflecting airflow, causing the aircraft to rotate around its vertical axis.

text: Identify the basic components of a hydraulic system.
knowledgeContent: Hydraulic systems include pumps, actuators, and fluid lines, used to transmit power for operating flight controls and landing gear.

text: Explain the principle of hydraulic power transmission.
knowledgeContent: Hydraulic systems use incompressible fluid to transmit force, enabling precise control of aircraft components.

text: Describe the function of hydraulic actuators.
knowledgeContent: Hydraulic actuators convert hydraulic pressure into mechanical motion, operating landing gear, flaps, and other systems.

text: Explain the importance of hydraulic fluid properties.
knowledgeContent: Hydraulic fluid must have low compressibility, high thermal stability, and resistance to corrosion for efficient system operation.

text: Describe the components of a fuel system.
knowledgeContent: Fuel systems include tanks, pumps, filters, and lines, ensuring a steady supply of fuel to the engine.

text: Explain the importance of fuel system management.
knowledgeContent: Proper fuel management ensures engine performance, prevents fuel starvation, and maintains aircraft balance.

text: Describe the purpose of fuel filters.
knowledgeContent: Fuel filters remove contaminants from the fuel, protecting the engine from damage and ensuring smooth operation.

text: Explain the function of fuel pumps.
knowledgeContent: Fuel pumps deliver fuel from the tanks to the engine at the required pressure, ensuring consistent fuel flow.

text: Identify the main components of an electrical system.
knowledgeContent: Electrical systems include batteries, alternators, and wiring, providing power for avionics, lighting, and other systems.

text: Describe the function of an aircraft alternator.
knowledgeContent: The alternator generates electrical power to recharge the battery and supply the aircraft’s electrical needs during flight.

text: Describe the purpose of circuit breakers in electrical systems.
knowledgeContent: Circuit breakers protect electrical circuits from overloads by interrupting current flow when excessive current is detected.

text: Explain the role of the battery in an electrical system.
knowledgeContent: The battery provides backup power and starts the engine, ensuring electrical continuity during alternator failure.

text: Compare piston and turboprop engine types.
knowledgeContent: Piston engines use reciprocating motion, while turboprop engines use a gas turbine to drive a propeller, offering higher efficiency at higher altitudes.

text: Explain the basic operation of a piston engine.
knowledgeContent: Piston engines convert fuel combustion into mechanical energy through a series of strokes: intake, compression, power, and exhaust.

text: Describe the advantages of turboprop engines.
knowledgeContent: Turboprop engines offer higher power-to-weight ratios and better fuel efficiency at medium altitudes compared to piston engines.

text: Explain the basic operation of a turboprop engine.
knowledgeContent: Turboprop engines use a gas turbine to drive a propeller, combining jet engine efficiency with propeller thrust.

text: Describe the function of a carburetor.
knowledgeContent: A carburetor mixes air and fuel in the correct ratio for combustion in a piston engine.

text: Explain the advantages of fuel injection systems.
knowledgeContent: Fuel injection systems provide precise fuel delivery, improving efficiency, performance, and reducing the risk of carburetor icing.

text: Describe the process of carburetor icing.
knowledgeContent: Carburetor icing occurs when moisture in the air freezes in the carburetor, restricting airflow and reducing engine performance.

text: Explain the operation of a fuel injection system.
knowledgeContent: Fuel injection systems deliver fuel directly into the engine cylinders or intake manifold, ensuring precise fuel-air mixture control.

text: Identify the components of an ignition system.
knowledgeContent: Ignition systems include spark plugs, magnetos, and wiring, providing the spark needed for fuel combustion in piston engines.

text: Describe the role of magnetos in engine operation.
knowledgeContent: Magnetos generate high-voltage electricity independently of the aircraft’s electrical system, ensuring reliable ignition.

text: Describe the function of spark plugs.
knowledgeContent: Spark plugs ignite the fuel-air mixture in the combustion chamber, initiating the power stroke in piston engines.

text: Explain the importance of ignition timing.
knowledgeContent: Proper ignition timing ensures efficient combustion, maximizing engine power and minimizing fuel consumption.

text: Explain the purpose of lubrication systems.
knowledgeContent: Lubrication systems reduce friction, cool engine components, and remove contaminants, ensuring smooth engine operation.

text: Describe the components of a lubrication system.
knowledgeContent: Lubrication systems include oil pumps, filters, and reservoirs, circulating oil to engine components.

text: Describe the purpose of oil filters.
knowledgeContent: Oil filters remove contaminants from the engine oil, protecting engine components from wear and damage.

text: Explain the function of oil coolers.
knowledgeContent: Oil coolers regulate engine oil temperature, preventing overheating and maintaining optimal lubrication.

text: Identify the types of propellers used in aircraft.
knowledgeContent: Fixed-pitch and variable-pitch propellers are common types. Fixed-pitch propellers have a set blade angle, while variable-pitch propellers allow adjustment for optimal performance.

text: Explain the operation of a variable-pitch propeller.
knowledgeContent: Variable-pitch propellers adjust blade angle to optimize thrust and efficiency across different flight conditions. This is typically controlled by a governor mechanism.

text: Describe the components of the pitot-static system.
knowledgeContent: The pitot-static system includes the pitot tube, static port, and associated tubing. It measures dynamic and static pressure for airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed calculations.

text: Explain the function of the altimeter.
knowledgeContent: The altimeter measures aircraft altitude by comparing static pressure to a reference pressure, typically set to QNH or QFE.

text: Describe how the airspeed indicator (ASI) works.
knowledgeContent: The ASI measures dynamic pressure from the pitot tube and static pressure from the static port to calculate indicated airspeed.

text: Explain the purpose of the vertical speed indicator (VSI).
knowledgeContent: The VSI measures the rate of climb or descent by detecting changes in static pressure over time.

text: Identify the gyroscopic instruments in an aircraft.
knowledgeContent: Common gyroscopic instruments include the attitude indicator (AI), heading indicator (DI), and turn coordinator.

text: Explain the operation of the attitude indicator (AI).
knowledgeContent: The AI uses a gyroscope to provide pitch and roll information relative to the horizon. It relies on rigidity in space and precession principles.

text: Describe the function of the heading indicator (DI).
knowledgeContent: The DI uses a gyroscope to display aircraft heading and must be periodically aligned with the magnetic compass.

text: Explain the purpose of the turn coordinator.
knowledgeContent: The turn coordinator indicates the rate of turn and coordination of a turn using a gyroscope and inclinometer.

text: Describe the operation of the magnetic compass.
knowledgeContent: The magnetic compass aligns with the Earth's magnetic field to indicate magnetic heading. It is subject to errors such as deviation and dip.

text: Identify common errors of the magnetic compass.
knowledgeContent: Errors include magnetic dip, deviation, and acceleration/turning errors. These can lead to inaccuracies in heading indication.

text: Explain the purpose of the cylinder head temperature (CHT) gauge.
knowledgeContent: The CHT gauge monitors the temperature of the engine cylinders to prevent overheating and ensure optimal engine performance.

text: Describe the function of the exhaust gas temperature (EGT) gauge.
knowledgeContent: The EGT gauge measures the temperature of exhaust gases to monitor combustion efficiency and fuel mixture.

text: Explain the role of the oil temperature and pressure gauges.
knowledgeContent: Oil temperature and pressure gauges monitor engine lubrication system health, ensuring proper oil flow and cooling.

text: Describe the purpose of the manifold pressure gauge.
knowledgeContent: The manifold pressure gauge measures the pressure in the engine intake manifold, indicating engine power output.

text: Identify the basic components of a VOR system.
knowledgeContent: A VOR system includes a ground station, antenna, and onboard receiver. It provides radial navigation information for aircraft.

text: Explain the function of an ADF system.
knowledgeContent: The ADF system uses ground-based NDBs to provide bearing information relative to the aircraft's position.

text: Describe the purpose of a transponder.
knowledgeContent: A transponder transmits aircraft identification and altitude information to air traffic control radar systems.

text: Explain the purpose of aircraft oxygen systems.
knowledgeContent: Oxygen systems provide supplemental oxygen to crew and passengers at high altitudes to prevent hypoxia.

text: Identify the types of oxygen systems used in aircraft.
knowledgeContent: Common types include continuous flow, demand flow, and pressure-demand systems, each suited for different altitudes and needs.

text: Describe the purpose of ice protection systems.
knowledgeContent: Ice protection systems prevent or remove ice accumulation on critical surfaces, ensuring safe flight in icing conditions.

text: Identify common types of ice protection systems.
knowledgeContent: Common systems include pneumatic de-icing boots, thermal heating, and chemical anti-icing fluids.

text: Explain the function of rain protection systems.
knowledgeContent: Rain protection systems, such as windshield wipers and hydrophobic coatings, maintain visibility during precipitation.

text: Describe the purpose of vacuum systems in aircraft.
knowledgeContent: Vacuum systems power gyroscopic instruments like the attitude indicator and heading indicator by creating suction.

text: Identify the components of a vacuum system.
knowledgeContent: Components include the vacuum pump, relief valve, filter, and associated tubing.

text: Explain the function of pressure systems in aircraft.
knowledgeContent: Pressure systems, such as pneumatic systems, provide power for instruments, landing gear, and other critical functions.
Understand the importance of weight and balance in aircraft performance and safety. Describe how improper loading affects stability and control.

text: Identify CG limits and their significance
knowledgeContent: Recognize the forward and aft CG limits for an aircraft. Explain how exceeding these limits impacts flight characteristics.
Use takeoff, landing, and climb performance charts to determine required distances and speeds. Recognize factors affecting performance (e.g., temperature, altitude).

text: Apply performance calculations for flight planning
knowledgeContent: Calculate takeoff and landing distances based on aircraft weight and environmental conditions. Adjust for obstacles and runway conditions.
Explain the purpose of the Certificate of Airworthiness (CofA) and Annual Review of Airworthiness (ARC). Identify who is responsible for maintaining these documents.
List mandatory inspections and maintenance tasks for airworthiness. Recognize the role of certified maintenance personnel.

text: Define key aircraft limitations (Vne, Vno, Va, Vfe, Vs)
knowledgeContent: Describe the meaning and implications of never-exceed speed (Vne), maneuvering speed (Va), and other critical speeds. Explain consequences of exceeding limits.
Use speed limitations during different flight phases (e.g., turbulence, flap operations). Recognize placards indicating operational limits.
Describe how stalls occur due to excessive angle of attack. Identify symptoms (buffeting, control loss) and recovery procedures.
Recognize conditions leading to spins (e.g., uncoordinated stall). Explain standard recovery techniques (reduce AOA, neutralize controls).
Demonstrate safe towing, parking, and securing of aircraft. Use proper techniques to avoid damage (e.g., wingtip clearance).
Recognize risks such as propeller strikes, ground loops, and jet blast. Apply precautions when operating near other aircraft.
Use approved methods and materials for cleaning exterior and interior surfaces. Avoid damage to sensitive components (e.g., pitot tubes).
Identify common corrosion types and areas prone to deterioration. Use protective coatings and storage best practices.
Locate and explain mandatory placards (e.g., fuel grades, passenger warnings). Recognize operational markings (e.g., gear limits, flap ranges).
Ensure aircraft operations align with placard instructions (e.g., weight limits, emergency procedures). Report discrepancies.
Locate and describe the use of fire extinguishers, emergency locator transmitters (ELTs), and life jackets. Check serviceability during pre-flight.
Operate ELTs and fire extinguishers in simulated emergencies. Explain life jacket donning procedures for passengers.

Explain magnetic deviation and its impact on compass readings.
Define isogonals and their significance in navigation charts.
Identify different types of aeronautical charts and their uses in navigation.
Recognize and interpret common symbols and abbreviations used on aeronautical charts.
Understand the concept of chart scales and their impact on navigation planning.
Identify and interpret topographic features on aeronautical charts.
Explain the principles of dead reckoning navigation and its application in flight planning.
Understand the wind triangle concept and its use in calculating heading and groundspeed.
Differentiate between track and heading, and explain how wind affects them.
Calculate drift angle and its impact on navigation.
Describe the basic steps involved in flight planning, including route selection and fuel calculation.
Understand the regulatory requirements for fuel reserves and their calculation.
Explain how weather conditions impact flight planning and navigation.
Identify the criteria for selecting alternate airports during flight planning.
Describe visual flight rules (VFR) navigation techniques, including pilotage and dead reckoning.
Explain the use of pilotage for navigation by reference to visible landmarks.
Identify common radio navigation aids and their use in VFR navigation.
Understand the principles of GPS navigation and its application in VFR flight.
Describe the basic principles of instrument flight rules (IFR) navigation.
Identify and interpret IFR charts, including enroute and approach charts.
Explain the different types of instrument approaches and their use in IFR navigation.
Understand the purpose and procedures for entering and flying holding patterns.
Explain the basic principles of radio navigation, including VOR, NDB, and DME.
Describe the operation and use of VOR (VHF Omnidirectional Range) for navigation.
Explain the operation and use of NDB (Non-Directional Beacon) for navigation.
Understand the use of DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) in navigation.
Explain the basic principles of GPS (Global Positioning System) and its components.
Understand the factors affecting GPS accuracy and the concept of selective availability.
Describe the creation and use of GPS waypoints in navigation.
Identify common GPS navigation errors and their mitigation.
Understand the different classes of airspace and their navigation requirements.
Explain the navigation requirements and procedures for operating in controlled airspace.
Describe the navigation considerations for operating in uncontrolled airspace.
Identify special use airspace and its impact on navigation planning.
Explain the purpose and content of a flight log in navigation.
Describe the process of completing a navigation log for flight planning.
Understand the importance of fuel logs in navigation and flight planning.
Explain the methods for monitoring flight progress during navigation.
Describe the procedures to follow if lost during navigation.
Explain the techniques for emergency navigation in case of equipment failure.
Understand the procedures for diverting to an alternate airport during navigation.
Describe the search and rescue (SAR) procedures relevant to navigation.
Identify the essential navigation equipment required for VFR and IFR flights.
Explain the pre-flight checks for navigation equipment.
Understand the procedures to follow in case of navigation equipment failure.
Describe the use of backup navigation methods and equipment.
Understand the regulatory requirements for navigation in VFR and IFR flights.
Explain the differences between VFR and IFR flight rules and their impact on navigation.
Describe the role of ATC communications in navigation.
Understand the procedures for obtaining and complying with navigation clearances.
Explain the considerations for international navigation, including customs and regulations.
Understand the ICAO standards and recommended practices for navigation.
Describe the procedures for crossing international borders during navigation.
Understand the language requirements for international navigation and ATC communications.
Understand the purpose and importance of pre-flight inspections. Know the key checks for airworthiness, fuel, oil, and control surfaces. Be aware of the consequences of skipping inspections.
Identify required documents for flight (e.g., Certificate of Airworthiness, registration, maintenance records). Understand pilot responsibilities regarding documentation checks.
Perform basic mass and balance calculations using aircraft manuals. Understand the effects of exceeding limits on aircraft performance and handling.
Recognize and interpret standard airport markings (runway, taxiway, holding points) and signs. Understand their significance for safe ground operations.
Identify airport lighting systems (runway, approach, taxiway). Understand pilot-controlled lighting and its operation.
Know procedures for operating at uncontrolled aerodromes. Understand radio procedures and traffic pattern entries.
Use standard ICAO radio phraseology correctly. Understand importance of clear communications in flight operations.
Recognize light and pyrotechnic signals from ATC. Know actions required in response to each signal type.
Understand factors determining aerodrome operating minima. Know how to assess weather conditions against minima.
Know documentation needed for flight planning (charts, NOTAMs, weather). Understand fuel reserve requirements.
Read and interpret NOTAMs relevant to flight operations. Identify critical information affecting the planned flight.
Apply VFR rules regarding visibility, cloud clearance, and airspace requirements. Know special VFR procedures.
Identify airspace classes A-G and their characteristics. Know equipment and clearance requirements for each class.
Understand how ATC uses radar and other surveillance. Know pilot responsibilities when receiving surveillance services.
Know what FIS provides and how to obtain it. Understand limitations of FIS compared to ATC services.
Understand ALERFA, DETRESFA, and INCERFA phases. Know when and how to declare emergencies.
Read back and comply with ATC clearances correctly. Know actions if unable to comply with clearance.
Understand basic separation minima applied by ATC. Know pilot responsibilities for maintaining separation.
Apply wake turbulence avoidance procedures during takeoff, landing, and enroute. Understand factors affecting wake vortex behavior.
Use see-and-avoid principle effectively. Know scanning techniques and collision avoidance maneuvers.
Enter and maintain visual holding patterns. Understand timing and wind correction techniques.
Recognize standard marshalling signals for ground operations. Know how to respond appropriately.
Apply procedures to prevent runway incursions. Use airport diagrams and maintain situational awareness.
Follow correct engine start checklist. Know fire prevention measures during start.
Taxi safely using proper techniques and checklists. Maintain awareness of other aircraft and obstacles.
Complete pre-takeoff checks systematically. Know critical items requiring immediate action if unsatisfactory.
Execute normal takeoff considering wind, runway, and aircraft performance. Use correct crosswind techniques.
Know conditions requiring rejected takeoff. Execute procedure safely within accelerate-stop distance.
Fly standard traffic patterns correctly. Apply proper altitude, spacing, and communication procedures.
Execute normal landing considering wind, runway, and aircraft configuration. Use proper flare and touchdown techniques.
Recognize situations requiring go-around. Execute procedure safely with proper power application and configuration changes.
Complete after-landing checks and taxi clear of runway promptly. Follow ATC instructions for parking.
Use emergency checklists systematically. Understand memory items requiring immediate action.
Use correct emergency radio phraseology (MAYDAY, PAN-PAN). Know when to squawk emergency codes.
Execute forced landing procedure selecting suitable landing area. Manage energy and configuration for safest outcome.
Handle engine, electrical, and cabin fires appropriately. Use correct extinguishing techniques and aircraft systems.
Execute emergency descent considering terrain and airspace. Use proper configuration and speed control.
Know correct procedures for refueling, oil servicing, and oxygen replenishment. Understand safety precautions during servicing.
Park aircraft considering wind, other traffic, and obstacles. Secure aircraft properly using tie-downs and controls locks.
Follow hangar safety procedures during aircraft movement and storage. Know fire prevention measures in hangars.
Identify temporary flight restrictions (TFRs) and prohibited areas. Know how to obtain current restriction information.
Recognize dangerous goods symbols and restrictions. Know reporting procedures for suspected dangerous goods incidents.
Apply basic aircraft security measures (cockpit door, baggage screening). Know procedures for suspicious items or behavior.
Know legal requirements for reporting accidents and incidents. Understand preservation of evidence at accident scene.
Understand consequences of airspace violations. Know procedures if unintentional violation occurs.
Understand PIC legal responsibilities regarding safety, documentation, and compliance with regulations.
Recognize effects of fatigue, stress, and medication on performance. Apply personal minimums and risk management.
Apply systematic decision-making models (e.g., DECIDE, 3P). Recognize and mitigate common decision-making errors.
Maintain situational awareness through systematic scanning and information processing. Recognize and recover from loss of SA.
Understand the composition of the atmosphere, including the percentages of nitrogen, oxygen, and other gases. Know how atmospheric pressure and density vary with altitude.
Define ISA conditions and understand how temperature, pressure, and density change with altitude in the ISA model. Know the ISA sea level values for temperature, pressure, and density.
Explain how pressure, density, and temperature are interrelated in the atmosphere. Understand how these properties affect aircraft performance.
Describe the airflow around a body, including the concepts of laminar and turbulent flow. Understand how airflow separation occurs and its effects.
Define the boundary layer and explain its characteristics. Understand how the boundary layer affects drag and lift.
Describe streamlines and how they represent airflow patterns. Understand how changes in airflow patterns affect lift and drag.
Explain how lift is generated on an airfoil using Bernoulli's principle and Newton's third law. Understand the factors affecting lift.

text: Angle of attack and lift coefficient
knowledgeContent: Define angle of attack and lift coefficient. Understand the relationship between angle of attack and lift, including the critical angle of attack.
Describe the conditions leading to a stall and the aerodynamic changes during a stall. Know the correct recovery procedure from a stall.
Identify and describe the different types of drag: parasite drag (form, friction, interference) and induced drag. Understand how each type affects aircraft performance.
Explain how drag varies with speed and the concept of total drag curve. Understand the implications for aircraft performance.
Describe methods to reduce drag, such as streamlining, winglets, and smooth surfaces. Understand how these methods improve efficiency.
Define thrust and power and explain how they are produced in piston and jet engines. Understand the relationship between thrust, power, and aircraft performance.
Describe how propeller efficiency is affected by factors such as blade angle, speed, and altitude. Understand the concept of fixed vs. variable pitch propellers.
Explain the relationship between thrust and drag in steady flight. Understand how changes in thrust affect climb, descent, and level flight.
Describe how weight and center of gravity affect aircraft stability and performance. Understand the importance of staying within weight and balance limits.
Define load factor and explain how it varies in different flight maneuvers. Understand the relationship between load factor and stall speed.
Describe the three types of stability: longitudinal, lateral, and directional. Understand how design features contribute to stability.
Explain the factors affecting glide performance, including best glide speed and glide ratio. Understand how to maximize glide distance.
Describe the factors affecting climb performance, including best rate and best angle of climb. Understand how altitude and weight affect climb.
Explain the forces involved in a turn and how bank angle affects turn radius and rate. Understand the relationship between load factor and stall speed in turns.
Describe the factors affecting takeoff performance, including runway length, wind, temperature, and aircraft weight. Understand the importance of rotation speed.
Explain the factors affecting landing performance, including approach speed, runway length, and wind. Understand the importance of flare and touchdown techniques.
Interpret aircraft performance charts for takeoff, climb, cruise, and landing. Understand how to adjust for non-standard conditions.
Describe the effects of high-speed flight, including compressibility and shock waves. Understand the concept of critical Mach number.
Explain the challenges of low-speed flight, including increased drag and reduced control effectiveness. Understand the importance of airspeed management.
Define ground effect and explain how it affects lift and drag during takeoff and landing. Understand the implications for aircraft handling.
Describe wind shear and its effects on aircraft performance and control. Understand how to recognize and respond to wind shear conditions.
Explain the causes and types of turbulence, including mechanical, thermal, and wake turbulence. Understand how to mitigate its effects.
Describe how icing affects aircraft performance, including lift, drag, and control surfaces. Understand the importance of anti-icing and de-icing systems.
Identify the primary flight controls (ailerons, elevator, rudder) and explain their functions. Understand how they affect aircraft movement.
Describe secondary flight controls (flaps, slats, trim) and their purposes. Understand how they enhance aircraft performance.
Explain how control surface effectiveness is affected by speed, airflow, and configuration. Understand the implications for aircraft handling.
Define adverse yaw and explain its causes. Understand how to compensate for adverse yaw using rudder and aileron coordination.
Describe spiral instability and its effects on aircraft control. Understand how to recognize and recover from a spiral dive.
Explain Dutch roll and its causes. Understand how yaw dampers and pilot inputs can mitigate Dutch roll.
Describe how wing design (aspect ratio, sweep, planform) affects lift and drag. Understand the trade-offs in wing design.
Identify high-lift devices (flaps, slats, slots) and explain how they increase lift. Understand their use in takeoff and landing.
Describe the purpose of vortex generators and how they improve airflow over wings and control surfaces. Understand their impact on performance.
Compare different aircraft configurations (high-wing, low-wing, monoplane, biplane) and their effects on stability and performance.
Explain how tail design (conventional, T-tail, V-tail) affects aircraft stability and control. Understand the advantages and disadvantages of each.
Describe different landing gear types (tricycle, tailwheel) and their effects on ground handling. Understand the challenges of each configuration.
Describe the aerodynamics of a spin, including the roles of yaw and roll. Understand the conditions required for a spin to develop.
Explain the correct procedure for spin recovery, including rudder, aileron, and elevator inputs. Understand the importance of altitude awareness.
Describe techniques to prevent spins, including proper stall recovery and coordinated flight. Understand the role of aircraft design in spin resistance.
Define maneuvering speed (Va) and explain its significance. Understand how weight affects maneuvering speed.
Describe the aerodynamic principles involved in aerobatic maneuvers (loops, rolls, spins). Understand the limitations and risks of aerobatic flight.
Explain the structural limits of an aircraft, including load factors and V-speeds. Understand the consequences of exceeding these limits.
Describe the aerodynamic principles specific to gliders, including lift-to-drag ratio and minimum sink rate. Understand how gliders maintain flight without an engine.
Explain how gliders use thermals, ridge lift, and wave lift to gain altitude. Understand the techniques for locating and exploiting lift sources.
Describe the factors affecting glider performance, including wing loading and polar curves. Understand how to optimize glide performance.
Explain the basic principles of balloon flight, including buoyancy and lift. Understand how temperature and altitude affect balloon performance.
Describe how balloons are controlled, including the use of burners and vents. Understand the limitations of balloon control in different weather conditions.
Explain the factors affecting balloon stability, including wind and thermal currents. Understand the challenges of maintaining stable flight in a balloon.
Describe how human factors, such as perception and reaction time, affect aerodynamic control. Understand the importance of training and awareness.
Explain how spatial disorientation can occur due to aerodynamic forces. Understand the techniques to prevent and recover from disorientation.
Describe pilot-induced oscillations (PIO) and their aerodynamic causes. Understand how to avoid and correct PIO.
Explain the sources of aerodynamic noise, including airflow over surfaces and propeller/engine noise. Understand how noise affects flight operations.
Describe how aerodynamic principles affect environmental considerations, such as fuel efficiency and emissions. Understand the role of aerodynamics in sustainable aviation.
Discuss emerging aerodynamic technologies, such as laminar flow control and blended wing bodies. Understand their potential impact on aviation.
Understand the role of communication in aviation safety and efficiency. Recognize the importance of clear, concise, and standardized phraseology.
Identify different types of aviation communication, including air traffic control (ATC), pilot-to-pilot, and ground communications. Understand the contexts in which each is used.
Explain the principles of radio telephony, including frequency bands, modulation, and line-of-sight propagation. Understand the importance of correct microphone technique.
Demonstrate the correct use of the ICAO phonetic alphabet for letters and numbers. Recognize its importance in avoiding miscommunication.
Apply standard phraseology as defined by ICAO and local ATC procedures. Understand deviations for non-standard situations.
Use correct transmission techniques, including speaking clearly, pausing appropriately, and confirming receipt of instructions.
Identify different ATC services (Tower, Approach, Area Control) and their respective responsibilities. Understand when to contact each service.
Describe the purpose and scope of Flight Information Services (FIS). Know how to request and interpret FIS broadcasts.
Perform a radio check and interpret the responses (e.g., 'Readability 1 to 5'). Know alternative methods if radio communication fails.
Identify the international emergency frequency (121.5 MHz) and other relevant emergency frequencies. Understand when and how to use them.
Execute correct initial contact procedures with ATC, including station identification, aircraft callsign, and position.
Apply readback procedures for ATC clearances and instructions, particularly for altitude, heading, and runway assignments.
Communicate effectively during ground movements, including taxi instructions, holding points, and runway crossings.
Follow correct procedures for departure communications, including startup, taxi, and takeoff clearances.
Communicate accurately while operating in the traffic circuit, including joining, position reports, and landing clearances.
Use correct communication procedures at uncontrolled aerodromes, including making blind broadcasts and listening out.
Provide accurate position reports during en-route flight, including essential elements (position, time, altitude, etc.).
Execute correct procedures for frequency changes, including when and how to contact the next ATC unit.
Request and maintain VFR flight following services. Understand the benefits and limitations of this service.
Make and interpret traffic information broadcasts (e.g., 'Traffic in sight'). Understand their role in collision avoidance.
Recall the priorities of emergency communications (Aviate, Navigate, Communicate). Understand when to declare an emergency.
Distinguish between MAYDAY (life-threatening) and PAN-PAN (urgent) situations. Know the correct format for these calls.
Follow prescribed procedures for radio failure, including squawking 7600 and using light signals or alternative methods.
Identify and use standard transponder codes (e.g., 7000 for VFR, 7600 for radio failure, 7700 for emergency).
Understand communication requirements for different airspace classes (A-G). Know when and how to establish contact.
Communicate effectively when operating near or through Temporary Restricted Areas (TRAs). Know how to obtain clearance if required.
Follow procedures for cross-border flights, including frequency changes and communication with foreign ATC units.
Understand ICAO language proficiency requirements for pilots. Know the consequences of not meeting the required level.
Obtain and interpret ATIS, AWOS, or ASOS broadcasts. Know how to report receiving the information to ATC.
Identify and use VOLMET and other aviation weather broadcasts. Understand their relevance to flight planning.
Request and interpret NOTAMs and other airspace information via communication with ATC or flight service stations.
Provide and interpret Pilot Reports (PIREPs) and airfield condition reports. Understand their importance for flight safety.
Recognize and respond to light signals from ATC or ground personnel, particularly in case of radio failure.
Identify and interpret visual ground signals, such as runway markings and marshalling signals.
Use and understand hand signals specific to glider operations, including launch and retrieval procedures.
Follow non-radio communication procedures at glider sites, including flag signals and other local practices.
Identify common causes of miscommunication in aviation (e.g., accents, similar callsigns). Apply strategies to mitigate these risks.
Understand how human factors (stress, fatigue, workload) affect communication effectiveness. Apply techniques to improve clarity.
Recognize how cultural differences may influence communication styles in international aviation. Adapt to ensure mutual understanding.
Apply techniques for effective communication when English is not the first language of either party. Use simplified phraseology if needed.
Perform pre-flight checks of radio equipment, including functionality testing and frequency selection.
Identify alternative communication methods (e.g., mobile phones, handheld radios) and their limitations in aviation.
Use communication procedures when interacting with radio navigation aids (e.g., requesting QDM, QNH).
Understand how to communicate with remote ATC facilities or AFIS units, including procedures for out-of-hours operations.
